scope.summary: Audited, intentional UI differences for the promoted Avalonia desktop screenshot pack relative to legacy Chummer5a mental models.

entries.parityIntent: Workbench-first startup posture with visible menu, core toolbar actions, and no wasted first-paint chrome.
evidenceAnchors: ShellMenuBarControl.axaml roots: FileMenuButton, EditMenuButton, SpecialMenuButton, ToolsMenuButton, WindowsMenuButton, HelpMenuButton.
ToolStripControl.axaml visible first-paint buttons: SaveButton, PrintButton, CopyButton, DesktopHomeButton, ImportFileButton, CloseWorkspaceButton, SettingsButton.

AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs hides ImportRawButton, LoadDemoRunnerButton, CampaignWorkspaceButton, UpdateStatusButton, InstallLinkingButton, SupportButton, and ReportIssueButton on the default workbench.
AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs keeps LeftNavigatorRegion, LoadedRunnerTabStripBorder, and RestoreContinuityStatusBorder collapsed before a runner is loaded.

differences.legacyPosture: Classic WinForms ToolStrip exposed New/Open/Save/Print/Copy as always-visible main-form actions tied to MDI-era chrome.
differences.currentPosture: The Avalonia startup strip keeps Save, Print, Copy, New, Open, Close, and Options as first-paint buttons inside a compact WrapPanel instead of duplicating the old ToolStrip widget stack.
differences.whyItDiffers: The user still gets one-click muscle memory for the first minute, but the promoted shell is tuned for a dense single-workbench layout rather than a literal WinForms clone.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy desktop utility affordances were spread across main-form chrome, menus, and detached helper flows.
differences.currentPosture: Those buttons still exist in the promoted head, but they stay hidden on the startup screenshot so the workbench does not burn first-paint width on demo, raw XML, campaign, update, linking, support, or bug chrome.
differences.whyItDiffers: The first screen is required to feel like Chummer's dense workbench, not a dashboard of secondary operations.

differences.legacyPosture: Chummer5a centered the first minute on the main form and did not spend initial vertical space on workspace rails, runner tabs, or recovery dossiers.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell mounts navigator, tab-strip, and restore controls in code but collapses them completely until a runner or recovery handoff makes them relevant.
differences.whyItDiffers: The modern shell needs those routes later, but startup parity is only credible if the dense workbench still owns the opening frame.

entries.parityIntent: Top-level command geography remains recognizable to a veteran in the first minute.
evidenceAnchors: ShellMenuBarControl.axaml declares FileMenuButton, EditMenuButton, SpecialMenuButton, ToolsMenuButton, WindowsMenuButton, and HelpMenuButton.
AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs asserts ClassicMenuLabels exactly equal File, Edit, Special, Tools, Windows, Help.
AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs requires FileMenuButton to surface open_character and save_character runtime commands.
CatalogOnlyRulesetShellCatalogResolver.cs and ShellChromeBoundary.cs bind current command ids open_character, global_settings, master_index, character_roster, and report_bug into the shell.

differences.legacyPosture: WinForms MenuStrip roots used the familiar File/Edit/Special/Tools/Windows/Help geography with ampersand accelerators and legacy chrome.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell keeps the same six roots by id and label, but they render through Avalonia menu primitives and modern shell styling instead of classic WinForms paint.
differences.whyItDiffers: Veteran navigation landmarks must survive, but the promoted head is not required to reproduce the old rendering engine's exact typography and accelerator quirks.

differences.legacyPosture: The legacy main form wired File/Open and File/Save directly to WinForms handlers.
differences.currentPosture: FileMenuButton now dispatches live command ids such as open_character and save_character through the promoted shell runtime instead of static form-event wiring.
differences.whyItDiffers: The new parity proof is only honest if the menu is backed by the current command pipeline rather than by a screenshot-only façade.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy menu geography was more static because the main form owned most command surfaces directly.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell keeps the familiar roots visible, but Edit and Special routes only expose live commands when the active workspace actually supports them and Windows no longer implies a full MDI manager.
differences.whyItDiffers: Current menus are required to stay truthful to the active workspace model instead of advertising dead or MDI-only paths.

entries.parityIntent: Global setup stays first-minute reachable and still reads like a serious desktop settings surface.
evidenceAnchors: AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs opens the same dialog from SettingsButton and Ctrl+G -> global_settings.
CommandDialogPaneControl.axaml exposes DialogTitleText, DialogFieldsHost, and DialogActionsHost for the inline settings surface.
DesktopDialogFactory.BuildGlobalSettingsFields creates globalSettingsTree, globalSettingsPropertyGrid, globalCharacterRosterPath, and globalPdfViewerPath.
AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs keeps FileMenuButton responsive while the Global Settings dialog is still mounted.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy settings were typically opened through dedicated menu or utility-form routes on the WinForms shell.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell routes both the toolbar button and the keyboard shortcut through the same global_settings command id and presenter path.
differences.whyItDiffers: Modern command routing must be consistent across toolbar, menu, and keyboard surfaces even when the visual destination remains recognizable.

differences.legacyPosture: EditGlobalSettings was a standalone WinForms window with its own frame, button row, and modality behavior.
differences.currentPosture: Global Settings is rendered inside the shared shell dialog host with a common title band, field stack, and action bar instead of a separate utility window.
differences.whyItDiffers: The promoted head standardizes dialog hosting so settings, import, utility, and builder dialogs all behave consistently inside the desktop shell.

differences.legacyPosture: The old settings form used WinForms tabs and heterogeneous controls to distribute options across several pages.
differences.currentPosture: The new dialog makes the navigation tree, current-pane property grid, roster path, PDF viewer path, and compact-mode fields explicit in one shared field model.
differences.whyItDiffers: The settings content still follows the old mental model, but it is represented through a structured dialog field system that the current shell can verify and reuse.

entries.parityIntent: A loaded runner still reads as a character-first workbench rather than a landing page or dashboard.
evidenceAnchors: AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs loads the bundled Soma runner through LoadDemoRunnerButton and then captures 04-loaded-runner-light.png.
LoadedRunnerTabStripBorder becomes visible after import while LeftNavigatorRegion stays collapsed for the single-runner posture.
LoadedRunnerTabStrip keeps stable info/profile and gear landmarks in SnapshotLoadedRunnerTabs().
SectionRowsList is visible and SectionPreviewBox is non-empty before the loaded-runner screenshot is accepted.

differences.legacyPosture: Chummer5a relied on frmCareer-era tab geography and form chrome to show that a runner was active.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted head uses a compact tab-strip card to expose the Runner/Profile/Gear landmarks inside the summary header instead of rebuilding legacy form tabs verbatim.
differences.whyItDiffers: The tab posture must still be obvious, but the current shell collapses everything that is not needed for a single loaded runner.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy navigation lived inside the main character form without a separate workspace rail concept.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell keeps workspace-rail plumbing out of sight for the default loaded-runner case and refuses to reserve width for an empty navigator strip.
differences.whyItDiffers: Single-runner parity is stronger when the workbench owns the available width instead of emulating multi-workspace chrome that is irrelevant in the screenshot.

differences.legacyPosture: frmCareer showed dense browse-and-detail landmarks through WinForms tabs, lists, and property-style panes.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell keeps the same dense list-and-detail rhythm, but it expresses it through a structured row list and a preview payload pane that the tests can snapshot directly.
differences.whyItDiffers: The behavioral landmark is the browse/detail cadence, not the old control class names.

entries.parityIntent: The light-theme dense builder keeps the same inspect, compare, and confirm rhythm without wasting space.
evidenceAnchors: Character_creation_preserves_familiar_dense_builder_rhythm() requires ClassicCharacterSheetBorder to be visible.
Gear_builder_preserves_familiar_browse_detail_confirm_rhythm() checks SectionRowsList rows gear.weapons[0] = Ares Alpha and gear.armor[0] = Armor Jacket.
SectionPreviewBox must contain combat/attributes payload markers before the dense screenshot is accepted.
NoticeBorder and NoticeText stay hidden so routine command noise does not consume visible workbench space.

differences.legacyPosture: frmCareer kept the runner summary woven into the character form rather than in detached cards or a landing dashboard.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell recreates that effect as a compact Runner Summary band inside SectionHostControl instead of copying the old form layout exactly.
differences.whyItDiffers: The familiarity target is a dense always-near summary, not a pixel match to every legacy panel edge.

differences.legacyPosture: Classic dense sections mixed WinForms lists, trees, and field groups to render gear and related rows.
differences.currentPosture: The Avalonia workbench renders rows as explicit DisplayPath/DisplayValue pairs such as gear.weapons[0] = Ares Alpha and gear.armor[0] = Armor Jacket.
differences.whyItDiffers: Path/value rows are more mechanically testable and still preserve the dense browse rhythm veterans expect.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy detail panes were embedded in WinForms sublayouts and were not exposed as a structured payload surface.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell keeps a hidden-but-mounted preview payload box that contains section markers such as combat and attributes for the selected row.
differences.whyItDiffers: The current head needs a stable, inspectable detail surface while still presenting a visually dense workbench.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy utility noise typically stayed out of the central editing pane unless the user explicitly triggered an alert.
differences.currentPosture: Routine command-dispatch notices are deliberately suppressed on the dense screenshot instead of occupying visible shell chrome.
differences.whyItDiffers: A loud status banner would break the frmCareer-style serious-work posture more than any missing pixel-perfect border would.

entries.parityIntent: Dark theme remains dense and readable without inheriting legacy dark-mode debt.
evidenceAnchors: Theme_tokens_preserve_chummer5a_palette_and_readability() asserts ChummerShellActiveMenuBorderBrush and ChummerShellAccentButtonBrush equal #1C4A2D in light and dark palettes.
The same test asserts ChummerShellActiveMenuBackgroundBrush is #1C4A2D and ChummerShellActiveMenuBorderBrush is #90C39A in dark mode.
The dark-theme proof enforces >=12:1 foreground contrast and >=7:1 muted-foreground contrast on shell surfaces.
06-dense-section-dark.png is captured from the same SectionRowsList workbench after ThemeVariant.Dark is applied.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy dark mode was inconsistent and inherited WinForms-era palette constraints.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell uses explicit branded dark tokens such as #1C4A2D and #90C39A instead of whatever the host toolkit would otherwise default to.
differences.whyItDiffers: Dark mode is allowed to improve materially because the parity contract is about familiar work rhythm, not preserving poor legacy color behavior.

differences.legacyPosture: The old client did not enforce hard contrast budgets across every dark surface.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted head hard-gates foreground, muted, warning, and danger contrast ratios in dark mode before the screenshot pack is accepted.
differences.whyItDiffers: Readable dense work matters more than cloning legacy contrast mistakes.

differences.uiElement: Dark-mode SectionRowsList and shell panel chrome
differences.legacyPosture: Dark workbench surfaces came from ad hoc panel fills and WinForms border behavior.
differences.currentPosture: The same dense list/detail surface is recolored through shared shell brushes and panel classes rather than per-form overrides.
differences.whyItDiffers: The promoted desktop needs one consistent dark-theme system across all major surfaces.

entries.parityIntent: Visible tab posture survives without reintroducing full MDI chrome.
evidenceAnchors: Loaded_runner_preserves_visible_character_tab_posture() requires LoadedRunnerTabStripBorder to be visible and to surface a Runner tab label.
Loaded_runner_header_stays_tab_panel_only_without_metric_cards() forbids NameValueText, AliasValueText, KarmaValueText, SkillsValueText, RuntimeValueText, and RuntimeInspectButton.
Desktop_shell_preserves_classic_dense_center_first_workbench_posture() requires WorkspaceStripRegion to stay absent and LeftNavigatorRegion width to collapse.
SnapshotLoadedRunnerTabs() is expected to contain info/profile and gear landmarks on the loaded runner.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy runner context was expressed through frmCareer-era tabs and main-form chrome.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted head distills that into a dedicated tab-strip card instead of an MDI-style shell or a second workspace row.
differences.whyItDiffers: Visible runner tabs are required; full legacy window-management chrome is not.

differences.legacyPosture: There was no separate modern workspace strip to keep alive for inactive sessions.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell explicitly collapses workspace-strip and left-rail chrome when only one runner matters.
differences.whyItDiffers: The central workbench is more important than showcasing future multi-workspace affordances on this proof.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy dense runner work did not stop above the workbench to read hero cards or metric dashboards.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell intentionally omits those card-style metrics from the header and leaves the header as tab chrome only.
differences.whyItDiffers: Any card stack here would feel less like Chummer5a than the absence of old border styling.

entries.parityIntent: Cyberware selection keeps the familiar browse-detail-confirm posture while making implant costs and essence easier to audit.
evidenceAnchors: AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs selects the row cyberware[0] = Wired Reflexes 2, confirms SectionPreviewBox contains essence 5.34, then clicks SectionQuickAction_cyberware_add.
DialogTitleText must equal Add Cyberware before 08-cyberware-dialog-light.png is captured.

DesktopDialogFactory.BuildCyberwareSelectionFields creates uiCyberwareCategoryTree, uiCyberwareCandidateList, uiCyberwareGrade, uiCyberwareEssence, uiCyberwareCost, uiCyberwareSource, and uiCyberwareSelectionDetails.
Cyberware dialogs expose add/add_more/cancel actions through BuildAddAndMoreActions().

differences.legacyPosture: Classic cyberware work launched from dedicated selector forms and context menus tied to legacy control trees.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell launches cyberware selection through a named section quick action anchored directly to the dense workbench row context.
differences.whyItDiffers: Quick actions keep the same user intent visible while fitting the shared section host model.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy dialogs relied on WinForms tree/list widgets and form-specific layout code for category browsing.
differences.currentPosture: The current dialog uses an explicit Navigation tree and Available Cyberware list field model with predictable ids and layout slots.
differences.whyItDiffers: The promoted shell needs selection dialogs that are both familiar to browse and mechanically testable across heads.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy implant modifiers and grade choices were spread across dialog-specific widgets that varied by selection form.
differences.currentPosture: The current dialog exposes grade, slot, rating, markup, and discount in a single shared field stack around the selected implant.
differences.whyItDiffers: The new field model makes the cost and essence implications easier to audit without hiding them behind form-specific control arrangements.

differences.legacyPosture: Older confirmation posture depended on bespoke totals areas and legacy button rows.
differences.currentPosture: Essence, cost, source, and selection details remain visible beside a standardized add/add more/cancel action bar.
differences.whyItDiffers: The current desktop puts explainability and reversible action posture ahead of faithfully cloning the old layout skeleton.

entries.parityIntent: Vehicles and drones stay dense, technical, and easy to re-find from the main workbench.
evidenceAnchors: Non_classic_sections_surface_a_named_workbench_context_instead_of_an_untitled_row_dump() requires SectionContextTitleText to equal Vehicles and SectionContextSummaryText to mention Roadmaster.
09-vehicles-section-light.png is captured after selecting the row vehicles[0] = Roadmaster in SectionRowsList.
Vehicles_and_drones_builder_preserves_familiar_browse_detail_confirm_rhythm() opens SectionQuickAction_vehicle_add with title Add Vehicle / Drone and field Vehicle.
DesktopDialogFactory.BuildVehicleSelectionFields creates uiVehicleCategoryTree, uiVehicleCandidateList, uiVehicleHandling, uiVehicleCost, uiVehicleSource, and uiVehicleSelectionDetails.

differences.legacyPosture: Vehicle work was often spread across dedicated forms or older nested layouts where the current context could be easy to lose.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted workbench adds an explicit Vehicles section header and summary line directly above the rows.
differences.whyItDiffers: Modern section context cues improve re-findability without changing the underlying builder rhythm.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy vehicle editors used form-specific grids and tab stacks for chassis state.
differences.currentPosture: Vehicle entries are rendered as dense path/value rows inside the same workbench list that drives the rest of the character builder.
differences.whyItDiffers: Keeping vehicles inside the shared row model reduces shell switching while still exposing the same dense editing cadence.

differences.legacyPosture: Add Vehicle or Drone often launched from per-form buttons tied to legacy vehicle tabs.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted section exposes vehicle creation as a named quick action attached to the current section host instead of a detached form button.
differences.whyItDiffers: Quick actions make the launch point consistent across builder families.

differences.legacyPosture: The legacy vehicle selector depended on form-specific tree and field arrangements.
differences.currentPosture: The current dialog keeps the same browse/detail/confirm loop, but it expresses navigation, candidate list, handling, cost, and source in a shared dialog field model.
differences.whyItDiffers: The shell can standardize the selection mechanics without erasing the vehicle-builder mental model.

entries.parityIntent: Contacts stay first-class, contextual, and editable without sending the user to a separate dashboard.
evidenceAnchors: 10-contacts-section-light.png is captured after SetActiveSectionForTesting("contacts") and selecting contacts[0] = Fixer in SectionRowsList.
SectionHostControl.axaml contains ContactGraphBorder, ContactNodeList, ContactFactionStatusBox, ContactHeatObligationBox, and ContactFavorRailBox for the contacts family.
Contacts_diary_and_support_routes_execute_with_public_path_visibility() opens contact_add with title Add Contact and required field Name.
DesktopDialogFactory.BuildContactAddFields creates uiContactName, uiContactRole, uiContactConnection, uiContactLoyalty, uiContactDetails, and uiContactNotes.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy contacts were edited through older field groups and control clusters separate from the main workbench narrative.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell keeps contacts in the same dense path/value row list used by other builder families, with the selected Fixer entry visible in-frame.
differences.whyItDiffers: The common row model improves re-findability and keeps contacts from feeling like a detached side utility.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy contact editing focused on direct form fields and less on adjacent relationship context surfaces.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted contacts lane can surface relationship graph, faction status, heat or obligation, and favor-rail context inside the same section host.
differences.whyItDiffers: Contacts are more useful when the surrounding social context is visible where the user is already editing.

differences.legacyPosture: Add Contact relied on legacy dialog controls arranged specifically for the old form stack.
differences.currentPosture: The current add-contact dialog keeps the same essential Name/Role/Connection/Loyalty fields but expresses them through the shared dialog field system.
differences.whyItDiffers: The shell can modernize how the fields are hosted without changing what a veteran needs to fill in.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy contact dialogs offered less structured summary context around the selected role and connection state.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted dialog pairs the edit fields with a Contact Details grid and Notes snippet so the selected social posture stays visible through confirmation.
differences.whyItDiffers: Current parity prioritizes explicit context over reproducing the exact old arrangement of textboxes and labels.

entries.parityIntent: Diary and progression-entry work stays compact, list-oriented, and tied to the active runner.
evidenceAnchors: AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs captures 11-diary-dialog-light.png after clicking SectionQuickAction_create_entry in the progress section.
DialogTitleText must equal Add Entry and the required field label is Entry Name before the screenshot is accepted.
DesktopDialogFactory.BuildEntryEditorFields creates uiEntrySections, uiCreateEntryName, uiEntryDetails, and uiEntryNotes.
Advancement_and_karma_journal_workflows_preserve_familiar_progression_rhythm() requires SectionPreviewBox to contain diary and karma markers.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy diary and expense flows opened through utility windows and per-form commands.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted head launches diary entry creation from the progress section's quick-action strip so the action starts in the same dense work context.
differences.whyItDiffers: The shared section host is the new center of gravity for editing, even when the underlying mental model remains diary-first.

differences.legacyPosture: The old flow used a standalone form with its own control arrangement for entry naming and details.
differences.currentPosture: The current dialog reduces that to an explicit Entry/Details/Notes section model plus a first-class Entry Name field.
differences.whyItDiffers: The dialog field contract is reused across the desktop shell, so the diary surface inherits that structure.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy entry utilities carried less structured inline explanation about current posture and list context.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted dialog keeps a Details grid and Notes snippet visible while the user creates the entry.
differences.whyItDiffers: Current parity favors explicit context and reversibility during edits.

differences.legacyPosture: Classic confirmation posture was expressed through dialog-specific button rows.
differences.currentPosture: The diary flow uses the shared add/add more/cancel action trio instead of custom button wiring.
differences.whyItDiffers: A common action contract makes shell behavior consistent across builder dialogs without changing the user's basic commit or cancel choices.

entries.parityIntent: Magic selection keeps obvious browse, inspect, and confirm landmarks while adapting to the current ruleset field model.
evidenceAnchors: AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs captures 12-magic-dialog-light.png after clicking SectionQuickAction_spell_add and waiting for DialogTitleText = Add Spell.
Magic_workflows_execute_with_specific_dialog_fields_and_confirm_actions() requires the field label Spell and the add action id.
DesktopDialogFactory.BuildSpellSelectionFields creates uiSpellCategoryTree, uiSpellCandidateList, uiSpellName, uiSpellSource, uiSpellSelectionDetails, and uiSpellNotes.
The same test family also exercises adept_power_add and initiation_add so the dialog contract is shared across magic-family routes.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy Add Spell flowed from frmCareer buttons and WinForms selector forms.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell launches Add Spell from a section quick action inside the dense workbench instead of a form-specific toolbar button.
differences.whyItDiffers: Magic actions are now routed through the shared section host so every builder family behaves consistently.

differences.legacyPosture: The old selector used legacy tree and list controls shaped by the WinForms form designer.
differences.currentPosture: The current dialog exposes an explicit spell category tree and Available Spells list with stable field ids and layout slots.
differences.whyItDiffers: The selection landmarks stay recognizable, but the structure must fit the shared dialog system.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy detail panels varied by form and did not share a common field contract across heads.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted dialog keeps Spell, Category, Source, and Selection Details visible in a single normalized field model.
differences.whyItDiffers: Current parity values testable structured context more than exact legacy control placement.

differences.legacyPosture: Classic spell dialogs had bespoke OK-style button rows.
differences.currentPosture: Add Spell uses the same add/add more/cancel action bar as other builder dialogs, even though the field content is magic-specific.
differences.whyItDiffers: The promoted shell standardizes action posture without erasing the spell-selection workflow.

entries.parityIntent: Matrix program selection stays technical and dense without leaving the main workbench shell model.
evidenceAnchors: AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs captures 13-matrix-dialog-light.png after clicking SectionQuickAction_matrix_program_add and waiting for Add Program / Cyberdeck Item.
Matrix_workflows_execute_with_specific_dialog_fields_and_confirm_actions() requires field label Program and action id add.

DesktopDialogFactory.BuildMatrixProgramSelectionFields creates uiMatrixProgramCategoryTree, uiMatrixProgramCandidateList, uiMatrixProgramName, uiMatrixProgramSlot, uiMatrixProgramSource, and uiMatrixProgramSelectionDetails.
The surrounding complexforms section also exposes complex_form_add, proving the matrix family shares one current shell lane rather than separate legacy forms.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy matrix editing used form-specific selectors and menu/button launch points tied to older control stacks.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell exposes Add Program / Cyberdeck Item as a named quick action inside the current section host.
differences.whyItDiffers: The workbench is now responsible for launching matrix flows instead of scattering them across detached selectors.

differences.legacyPosture: Older dialogs used legacy selector widgets to split programs, deck items, and dongles.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted dialog keeps the same distinction, but it expresses it through an explicit Navigation tree and Available Programs list.
differences.whyItDiffers: The shell can standardize the dialog framework while still preserving the technical browse posture.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy detail panes were layout-specific and less uniform across matrix subflows.
differences.currentPosture: Program name, slot, source, and selection details remain visible as named fields inside the shared dialog contract.
differences.whyItDiffers: Matrix parity depends on visible slot and source context, not on keeping the old field arrangement byte-for-byte.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy matrix selectors used custom button rows and form-event wiring.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted matrix dialog uses the same standardized add/add more/cancel action bar as the rest of the builder family.
differences.whyItDiffers: Consistent action posture across builders is a deliberate modern shell choice.

entries.parityIntent: Advancement stays tied to visible karma history and still makes grade or reward choices explicit before commit.
evidenceAnchors: Advancement_and_karma_journal_workflows_preserve_familiar_progression_rhythm() requires progress[0] = First extraction · +2 karma in SectionRowsList and diary/karma markers in SectionPreviewBox.
AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs captures 14-advancement-dialog-light.png after clicking SectionQuickAction_initiation_add and waiting for Add Initiation / Submersion.
The same test requires field label Grade and action id add.
DesktopDialogFactory.BuildInitiationSelectionFields creates uiInitiationTrack, uiInitiationGrade, uiInitiationCandidateList, uiInitiationReward, uiInitiationSelectionDetails, and uiInitiationNotes.

differences.uiElement: SectionRowsList progress[0] = First extraction · +2 karma / SectionPreviewBox diary+karma payload
differences.legacyPosture: Legacy advancement cues were distributed across expense, karma, and character forms.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted workbench keeps the current progression row and diary or karma payload visible directly in the progress section before the dialog opens.
differences.whyItDiffers: Advancement parity is stronger when the running history remains visible in the same lane as the next action.

differences.legacyPosture: Initiation and submersion choices launched from legacy buttons and selector forms tied to older section layouts.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted head launches the initiation flow from the progress workbench through a named quick action.
differences.whyItDiffers: The quick-action model keeps advancement tools attached to the same dense editing lane.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy initiation dialogs used form-specific controls to mix track, grade, and reward selection.
differences.currentPosture: The current dialog exposes Track, Grade, Available Rewards, and Reward as normalized fields inside the shared dialog shell.
differences.whyItDiffers: The shell now needs a stable field contract across advancement flows while preserving the same user decisions.

differences.legacyPosture: Classic advancement flows depended on dialog-specific OK/Cancel rows.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted dialog uses the shared add/add more/cancel bar after keeping grade and metamagic or echo context visible.
differences.whyItDiffers: Standardized commit posture reduces shell variation without changing the core progression workflow.

entries.parityIntent: Character creation still feels like a dense builder, not a wizard or marketing surface.
evidenceAnchors: Character_creation_preserves_familiar_dense_builder_rhythm() requires ClassicCharacterSheetBorder to be visible in the creation-like surface.
The same test requires SectionRowsList to contain attributes.body = 5, attributes.agility = 7, and skills.firearms[0] = Automatics 6.
SectionPreviewBox must contain attributes and combat markers before the screenshot is accepted.
15-creation-section-light.png is captured after SetActiveSectionForTesting("attributes") on the loaded-runner workbench, not from a detached wizard window.

differences.legacyPosture: CharacterCreate used dedicated creation-form controls, pages, and widgets to expose attributes and skills.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted builder renders creation facts as dense path/value rows inside the same workbench row list used elsewhere.
differences.whyItDiffers: The dense builder feel survives even though the control implementation is unified with the rest of the current shell.

differences.legacyPosture: The old creation experience kept a compact runner summary close to the editing surface rather than delegating it to an external dashboard.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted creation surface uses the same Runner Summary band to keep the character sheet posture visible while editing attributes.
differences.whyItDiffers: One summary-band implementation is cheaper to maintain and still gives the user the same dense builder cue.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy creation panes expressed detail context through form-specific fields and labels.
differences.currentPosture: The current builder keeps a structured preview payload with attributes and combat markers mounted alongside the dense list.
differences.whyItDiffers: The preview surface makes the current builder auditable without forcing a separate wizard step.

differences.uiElement: Loaded-runner workbench hosting instead of a detached creation form
differences.legacyPosture: Character creation lived in a separate dedicated WinForms experience.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted head hosts creation-like editing inside the same dense workbench shell and does not bounce the user into a separate wizard frame for this proof.
differences.whyItDiffers: The modern shell is intentionally consolidated, but it must still feel like a serious creation builder.

entries.parityIntent: Master Index remains a first-class searchable utility with stronger source provenance than the legacy form.
evidenceAnchors: AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs opens ToolsMenuButton -> master_index and captures the dialog after AssertDialogContainsAll(..., ["Master Index"]).
DesktopDialogFactory.BuildMasterIndexFields creates masterIndexSections, masterIndexSearch, masterIndexCatalogEntries, masterIndexDetails, masterIndexSnippetPreview, and masterIndexSelectedSource.
The same field builder also creates masterIndexReferenceCoverage, masterIndexReferenceSources, and masterIndexReferenceSourceReceipt.
The dialog closes through the shared close action instead of a standalone utility-form frame.

differences.legacyPosture: Master Index was a named WinForms utility surfaced from the legacy tools lane.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell preserves the same named utility route, but it opens inside the shared dialog host rather than a separate form window.
differences.whyItDiffers: The route must stay familiar even though utility hosting is now standardized.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy Master Index centered on a dedicated utility form with its own search and result widgets.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted dialog keeps Sections, Search, and Items explicit as named fields inside the shared dialog contract.
differences.whyItDiffers: The shared dialog model gives the current shell stable, testable field ids instead of form-specific widget wiring.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy Master Index focused on local reference navigation and did not consistently surface snippet provenance in one normalized panel.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted dialog keeps Details, Snippet Preview, and Source visible together so the selected sourcebook's current provenance is explicit.
differences.whyItDiffers: Reference truth is now a governed product contract, not just a convenience utility.

differences.legacyPosture: The old Master Index did not expose modern receipt-style coverage and source-governance fields.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted dialog surfaces snippet coverage, reference-source posture, and a reference-source receipt as first-class fields.
differences.whyItDiffers: The flagship desktop now has to prove source quality and provenance, not merely show that search results exist.

entries.parityIntent: Character Roster remains a named first-class utility for multi-runner context.
evidenceAnchors: AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs opens ToolsMenuButton -> character_roster and captures the dialog after AssertDialogContainsAll(..., ["Character Roster"]).
DesktopDialogFactory.BuildRosterFields creates rosterTree, rosterEntries, rosterSelectedRunner, rosterMugshot, rosterSelectedRunnerStatus, and rosterSelectedRunnerNotes.
The same builder also creates rosterOpenCount, rosterSavedCount, rosterRulesetMix, and rosterActiveWorkspace.
The roster dialog closes through the shared close action instead of a separate utility window frame.

differences.legacyPosture: Character Roster was a standalone WinForms utility reachable from the legacy tools lane.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell keeps the named Character Roster route but hosts it inside the shared dialog layer.
differences.whyItDiffers: The route stays veteran-familiar while the window-hosting model is intentionally modernized.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy roster views depended on utility-form trees and lists with form-specific layout rules.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted dialog renders Characters and Roster Entries as explicit shared fields, including open-runner ordering and save posture markers.
differences.whyItDiffers: The field contract is reusable across heads and lets the shell verify multi-runner state directly.

differences.legacyPosture: The old roster centered on the runner list and supporting form fields without the same normalized summary model.
differences.currentPosture: The current roster keeps selected-runner summary, mugshot placeholder, status snippet, and notes snippet visible in one standardized detail region.
differences.whyItDiffers: Current roster parity needs stronger explicit context about the selected runner and active workspace.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy roster utilities did not expose modern session-level counts and ruleset-mix posture as first-class dialog fields.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted roster makes open runner count, saved workspace count, ruleset mix, and active workspace explicit at the top of the dialog.
differences.whyItDiffers: The current shell has to explain the session state around the roster, not only render the character list.

entries.parityIntent: Import still begins from File/Open posture while making current ruleset and payload expectations explicit.
evidenceAnchors: AvaloniaFlagshipUiGateTests.cs loads the demo runner, opens FileMenuButton, dispatches open_character, and then captures the dialog after AssertDialogContainsAll(..., ["Open Character"]).
DesktopDialogFactory.CreateOpenCharacterDialog creates importRulesetId and openCharacterXml fields with title Open Character and message Paste Chummer XML to import into a workspace.
The dialog exposes import/cancel actions through the shared dialog action model.
MainWindow.DesktopFileCoordinator.cs still carries the host-facing title Open Character File for the desktop file route.

differences.legacyPosture: Veterans expect import to begin from the File/Open route on the main desktop shell.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted head keeps that exact shell entry, but it proves it through a runtime command id instead of a legacy form handler.
differences.whyItDiffers: The command pipeline changed, but the user's first step is intentionally still File/Open.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy import flows relied on older file-picker or importer-specific forms without a normalized shared field contract.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted dialog makes Ruleset and Character XML explicit named fields inside the shell dialog model.
differences.whyItDiffers: The current desktop needs a consistent import surface that can be audited and reused across related routes.

differences.legacyPosture: Classic import dialogs used form-specific buttons and callback wiring.
differences.currentPosture: Import uses the shared dialog action model with an explicit import primary action and cancel secondary action.
differences.whyItDiffers: Unified action handling is a deliberate shell-level simplification, not a loss of import capability.

differences.legacyPosture: Legacy import blended desktop-file and importer behavior directly into WinForms main-form flows.
differences.currentPosture: The promoted shell keeps a host-facing Open Character File route while the in-shell dialog focuses on ruleset and payload review.
differences.whyItDiffers: Desktop file integration and in-shell import confirmation are intentionally separated in the current architecture.
